result.markdown: --- title: "Universal Differential Equations: Learning Unknown Mechanisms" subtitle: "Combining mechanistic models with machine learning - good luck to us!" format: html engine: julia --- # Introduction Throughout this course, we've assumed we know the functional form of disease transmission: $$\beta \cdot \frac{S \cdot I}{N}$$ But what if we don't? What if: - **Transmission depends on behavior changes** we can't model explicitly - **Environmental factors** play an unknown role - **Contact patterns** are more complex than mass action - **Interventions** have effects we don't fully understand **Universal Differential Equations (UDEs)** offer a radical solution: **Let the data tell us the functional form!** ## The UDE approach Keep the **known** epidemiological structure: ``` dS/dt = -transmission(S, I, t) # ← Learn this part! dE/dt = +transmission(S, I, t) - σ * E dI/dt = +σ * E - γ * I ... ``` Replace unknown functions with **neural networks**: ``` transmission(S, I, t) = NeuralNetwork([I/N, t]) * S/N ``` ## Learning objectives By the end of this session, you will: 1. Understand when and why to use UDEs 2. Implement a simple UDE for epidemic modeling 3. Regularize neural networks for sparse data (~60 points) 4. Compare UDE vs fixed transmission assumptions 5. Appreciate the challenges ("good luck to us!") **Warning**: This is cutting-edge research! Expect: - ✅ Cool concepts and elegant code - ⚠️ Hyperparameter tuning required - ❌ Not always better than simple models - 🎓 Great learning experience even when it doesn't work perfectly! # Setup ::: {#4 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} using DifferentialEquations using Distributions using DataFrames using CSV using Plots using Random using LinearAlgebra using DrWatson using Lux # Modern neural network framework using ComponentArrays using Optimization using OptimizationOptimisers using OptimizationOptimJL using Zygote using Optimisers # For Adam optimizer Random.seed!(1234) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ``` TaskLocalRNG() ``` ::: ::: # Load Tristan da Cunha data ::: {#6 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} flu_tdc = CSV.read(datadir("flu_tdc_1971.csv"), DataFrame) scatter( flu_tdc.time, flu_tdc.obs, xlabel = "Time (days)", ylabel = "Daily incidence", label = "Observed cases", markersize = 4, color = :red, title = "Tristan da Cunha 1971: Can we learn β(t)?", legend = :topright ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ```{=html} ``` ::: ::: **Challenge**: With only ~60 data points, can we learn a time-varying transmission rate? # Baseline: SIR with constant β First, let's establish what a constant transmission rate gives us: ::: {#8 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} """ Standard SIR model with constant transmission. """ function sir_constant_beta(du, u, p, t) S, I, R = u β, γ = p N = S + I + R du[1] = -β * S * I / N du[2] = β * S * I / N - γ * I du[3] = γ * I end # Fit by eye (we'd use MCMC in reality, but we'll keep this quick) β_guess = 0.5 γ_guess = 0.4 u0 = [270.0, 3.0, 0.0] tspan = (0.0, Float64(maximum(flu_tdc.time))) times = Float64.(flu_tdc.time) prob_constant = ODEProblem(sir_constant_beta, u0, tspan, [β_guess, γ_guess]) sol_constant = solve(prob_constant, Tsit5(), saveat=times) # Extract incidence S_values = [sol_constant(t)[1] for t in times] incidence_constant = [0.0; -diff(S_values)] # Plot plot(times, incidence_constant, label="Constant β", linewidth=2) scatter!(flu_tdc.time, flu_tdc.obs, label="Observed", markersize=4, color=:red, xlabel="Time (days)", ylabel="Cases", title="Baseline: Can we do better?") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ```{=html} ``` ::: ::: **Question**: The fit isn't perfect. Could a time-varying β(t) do better? # UDE Approach: Learn β(t) with Neural Network ## Step 1: Define the neural network We'll use a **small** network - remember, we only have 60 data points! ::: {#10 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} # Network architecture: 2 inputs → 16 hidden → 8 hidden → 1 output # Total parameters: ~150 (small enough for 60 data points) rng = Random.default_rng() Random.seed!(rng, 1234) # Define network structure nn_architecture = Chain( Dense(2 => 16, tanh), # Input: [I/N, t/tmax] Dense(16 => 8, tanh), Dense(8 => 1, softplus) # Output: β > 0 (softplus ensures positivity) ) # Initialize parameters p_nn, st_nn = Lux.setup(rng, nn_architecture) p_nn = ComponentArray(p_nn) # Convert to ComponentArray for optimization println("Neural network parameters: ", length(p_nn)) println("Data points: ", length(flu_tdc.obs)) println("Ratio: ", round(length(flu_tdc.obs) / length(p_nn), digits=2), " data points per parameter") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Neural network parameters: 193 Data points: 59 Ratio: 0.31 data points per parameter ``` ::: ::: **Critical**: With ~0.4 data points per parameter, we're in the **high risk overfitting zone**! We'll need **strong regularization**. ## Step 2: Define UDE with learned β(t) ::: {#12 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} """ SIR model where β is learned by neural network. β = NN([I/N, t/tmax]) predicts transmission rate. """ function sir_ude!(du, u, p, t) S, I, R = u N = S + I + R γ = p.γ # Keep recovery rate fixed (known from literature) # Neural network predicts β(t, I) # Inputs: prevalence (I/N) and normalized time (t/tmax) tmax = p.tmax nn_input = [I/N, t/tmax] # Evaluate neural network β_learned, _ = nn_architecture(nn_input, p.nn_params, st_nn) β = β_learned[1] # Extract scalar # Standard SIR dynamics with learned β du[1] = -β * S * I / N du[2] = β * S * I / N - γ * I du[3] = γ * I end ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ``` Main.Notebook.sir_ude! ``` ::: ::: ## Step 3: Prediction and loss function ::: {#14 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} """ Predict incidence using current UDE parameters. """ function predict_ude(p_all) # Solve ODE with current parameters prob_ude = ODEProblem(sir_ude!, u0, tspan, p_all) sol_ude = solve(prob_ude, Tsit5(), saveat=times, dense=false) # Extract incidence if sol_ude.retcode != :Success return fill(NaN, length(times)) end S_pred = [sol_ude.u[i][1] for i in 1:length(times)] incidence_pred = [0.0; -diff(S_pred)] return incidence_pred end """ Loss function with HEAVY regularization for sparse data. """ function loss_ude(p_all) pred = predict_ude(p_all) # Data fit term: sum of squared errors data_loss = sum((pred .- flu_tdc.obs).^2) # REGULARIZATION (critical for 60 data points!) # 1. L2 penalty on NN weights (prevent large weights) l2_penalty = 1e-4 * sum(abs2, p_all.nn_params) # 2. Smoothness: penalize rapid changes in β(t) # Sample β at many time points and penalize differences t_sample = range(0.0, p_all.tmax, length=50) I_sample = 10.0 # Representative I value β_samples = [begin input = [I_sample / 273.0, t / p_all.tmax] β_val, _ = nn_architecture(input, p_all.nn_params, st_nn) β_val[1] end for t in t_sample] smoothness_penalty = 1e-3 * sum(abs2, diff(β_samples)) # 3. Bounded β: penalize if β > 2.0 (biologically unrealistic) bound_penalty = 1e-2 * sum(max.(β_samples .- 2.0, 0.0).^2) total_loss = data_loss + l2_penalty + smoothness_penalty + bound_penalty return total_loss end ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ``` Main.Notebook.loss_ude ``` ::: ::: **Note the regularization weights** (1e-4, 1e-3, 1e-2): These are **critical** hyperparameters! - Too weak → overfitting - Too strong → underfitting - Just right → "good luck to us!" 😄 ## Step 4: Train the UDE This is where the magic (and pain) happens: ::: {.callout-warning} ## Computationally Expensive **This training block is disabled in CI builds** to avoid timeouts. Students can run it locally by removing the `#| eval: false` line. For full training: - Use `maxiters=500` (Adam) and `maxiters=100` (LBFGS) - Takes ~5-10 minutes total - For a quick test, use smaller iterations (50 and 20) ::: ::: {#16 .cell execution_count=0} ``` {.julia .cell-code} # Combine all parameters p_all = ComponentArray( nn_params = p_nn, γ = γ_guess, tmax = maximum(times) ) println("Starting UDE training (DEMO VERSION - 30 total iterations)...") println("For real training, increase maxiters to 500 and 100") println("="^60) # Initial loss println("Initial loss: ", round(loss_ude(p_all), digits=2)) # Stage 1: Adam optimizer (exploration) - DEMO: only 20 iterations println("\nStage 1: Adam optimization (20 iterations - DEMO)") opt_func = OptimizationFunction((x, p) -> loss_ude(x), Optimization.AutoZygote()) opt_prob = OptimizationProblem(opt_func, p_all) @time result_adam = solve(opt_prob, Optimisers.Adam(0.01), maxiters=20, callback = (state, l) -> begin if state.iter % 5 == 0 println(" Iter $(state.iter): loss = $(round(l, digits=2))") end return false end) println("\nAfter Adam: loss = ", round(result_adam.objective, digits=2)) # Stage 2: LBFGS (refinement) - DEMO: only 10 iterations println("\nStage 2: LBFGS optimization (10 iterations - DEMO)") opt_prob2 = remake(opt_prob, u0=result_adam.u) @time result_lbfgs = solve(opt_prob2, LBFGS(), maxiters=10, callback = (state, l) -> begin if state.iter % 5 == 0 println(" Iter $(state.iter): loss = $(round(l, digits=2))") end return false end) println("\n" * "="^60) println("Final loss: ", round(result_lbfgs.objective, digits=2)) println("Training complete! (NOTE: This was demo version - increase iterations for real results)") p_trained = result_lbfgs.u ``` ::: **Expected behavior** (with full training): - Loss should decrease significantly (from ~100000 to ~1000-5000) - Full training takes 5-10 minutes with 500+100 iterations - Demo version shows the code works but won't converge fully - Final loss may still be higher than you'd like (60 points is tough!) ## Step 5: Compare results ::: {#18 .cell execution_count=0} ``` {.julia .cell-code} # Get UDE predictions pred_ude = predict_ude(p_trained) # Plot comparison plot(times, incidence_constant, label="Constant β", linewidth=2, xlabel="Time (days)", ylabel="Cases", title="UDE vs Constant β") plot!(times, pred_ude, label="UDE (learned β)", linewidth=2) scatter!(flu_tdc.time, flu_tdc.obs, label="Observed", markersize=4, color=:red) ``` ::: **Question**: Did the UDE fit better? Or did it just overfit? ## Step 6: Visualize learned β(t) The coolest part - what did the network learn? ::: {#20 .cell execution_count=0} ``` {.julia .cell-code} # Extract learned β(t) for representative I values t_plot = range(0.0, maximum(times), length=100) I_representative = 50.0 # Mid-epidemic prevalence β_learned_over_time = [begin input = [I_representative / 273.0, t / maximum(times)] β_val, _ = nn_architecture(input, p_trained.nn_params, st_nn) β_val[1] end for t in t_plot] # Plot plot(t_plot, β_learned_over_time, label="Learned β(t)", linewidth=2, xlabel="Time (days)", ylabel="β", title="What the Neural Network Learned") hline!([β_guess], label="Constant β baseline", linestyle=:dash) ``` ::: **Interpretation**: - If β(t) is roughly constant → UDE confirms simple model - If β(t) varies smoothly → Might capture real behavioral changes - If β(t) is erratic → Probably overfitting! # The "Good Luck to Us!" Section ## Why is this hard? 1. **Small dataset** (60 points) vs **many parameters** (150) 2. **Identifiability**: Many β(t) curves could fit the data 3. **Regularization tuning**: Finding the right penalties is an art 4. **Initialization**: Different random seeds → different solutions 5. **ODE stiffness**: Training can be numerically unstable ## Common failure modes ### Overfitting ```julia # If your loss is very low but predictions look crazy → overfitting # Solution: Increase regularization (1e-3 → 1e-2) ``` ### Underfitting ```julia # If UDE ≈ constant β → network learned nothing # Solution: Decrease regularization (1e-3 → 1e-4) # Or increase network capacity ``` ### NaN explosion ```julia # If you get NaN errors → numerical instability # Solution: Reduce learning rate (0.01 → 0.001) # Add gradient clipping # Use more robust ODE solver ``` ## Sensitivity to hyperparameters Let's test if our solution is robust by trying different regularization strengths. ::: {.callout-note} ## Quick Demo **Test 1 shows a quick demo** (15 iterations) to demonstrate sensitivity testing. For thorough analysis, students should run all three tests with more iterations (change `maxiters=15` → `200`). ::: **Test 1: Lower regularization (0.5×)** ::: {#22 .cell execution_count=0} ``` {.julia .cell-code} println("Testing regularization scale: 0.5×") # Modify loss function - less regularization function loss_test_low(p) pred = predict_ude(p) data_loss = sum((pred .- flu_tdc.obs).^2) l2_pen = (1e-4 * 0.5) * sum(abs2, p.nn_params) return data_loss + l2_pen end opt_func_test = OptimizationFunction((x, p) -> loss_test_low(x), Optimization.AutoZygote()) opt_prob_test = OptimizationProblem(opt_func_test, p_all) result_test_low = solve(opt_prob_test, Optimisers.Adam(0.01), maxiters=15, callback = (state, l) -> begin if state.iter % 5 == 0 println(" Iter $(state.iter): loss = $(round(l, digits=2))") end return false end) println("Final loss (0.5×): ", round(result_test_low.objective, digits=2)) println("(To see sensitivity, compare with tests 2 and 3 below)") ``` ::: **Test 2: Baseline regularization (1.0×)** ::: {#24 .cell execution_count=0} ``` {.julia .cell-code} println("Testing regularization scale: 1.0×") function loss_test_baseline(p) pred = predict_ude(p) data_loss = sum((pred .- flu_tdc.obs).^2) l2_pen = (1e-4 * 1.0) * sum(abs2, p.nn_params) return data_loss + l2_pen end opt_func_test = OptimizationFunction((x, p) -> loss_test_baseline(x), Optimization.AutoZygote()) opt_prob_test = OptimizationProblem(opt_func_test, p_all) result_test_baseline = solve(opt_prob_test, Optimisers.Adam(0.01), maxiters=200, callback = (state, l) -> begin if state.iter % 50 == 0 println(" Iter $(state.iter): loss = $(round(l, digits=2))") end return false end) println("Final loss (1.0×): ", round(result_test_baseline.objective, digits=2)) ``` ::: **Test 3: Higher regularization (2.0×)** ::: {#26 .cell execution_count=0} ``` {.julia .cell-code} println("Testing regularization scale: 2.0×") function loss_test_high(p) pred = predict_ude(p) data_loss = sum((pred .- flu_tdc.obs).^2) l2_pen = (1e-4 * 2.0) * sum(abs2, p.nn_params) return data_loss + l2_pen end opt_func_test = OptimizationFunction((x, p) -> loss_test_high(x), Optimization.AutoZygote()) opt_prob_test = OptimizationProblem(opt_func_test, p_all) result_test_high = solve(opt_prob_test, Optimisers.Adam(0.01), maxiters=200, callback = (state, l) -> begin if state.iter % 50 == 0 println(" Iter $(state.iter): loss = $(round(l, digits=2))") end return false end) println("Final loss (2.0×): ", round(result_test_high.objective, digits=2)) ``` ::: **If results vary wildly** → Model is unstable (not unusual with 60 points!) # When to use UDEs ## Use UDEs when: ✅ You have **mechanistic knowledge** about structure (compartments) ✅ But **unknown functional forms** (transmission, observation process) ✅ You have **moderate-to-large datasets** (ideally 100s-1000s of points) ✅ You're willing to **experiment** and **tune hyperparameters** ✅ **Exploratory analysis** - discovering unknown mechanisms ## Don't use UDEs when: ❌ Simple parametric models work fine (Occam's razor!) ❌ Very small datasets (<50 points) without strong regularization ❌ You need **interpretable** parameters for publication ❌ Computational resources are limited (training is expensive) ❌ Real-time inference needed (MCMC may be faster per dataset) # Comparison with other approaches | Method | Flexibility | Interpretability | Data needs | Computational cost | |--------|-------------|------------------|------------|-------------------| | **Constant β** | Low | High | Low (10-50 points) | Very low | | **Time-varying β with splines** | Medium | Medium | Medium (50-200) | Low | | **GP for β(t)** | Medium | High | Medium (50-200) | Medium | | **UDE** | High | Low | High (100-1000+) | High | **For TdC data (60 points)**: GPs or splines likely better than UDEs! # Real success stories UDEs work well when: 1. **Large spatiotemporal datasets**: COVID-19 with daily data from 100+ locations 2. **High-frequency measurements**: Lab experiments with measurements every hour 3. **Known structure, unknown details**: Chemical reactions, climate models 4. **Discovery research**: Finding unknown mechanisms (not just fitting) # Exercises 1. **Network size**: Try smaller (8→4→1) and larger (32→16→1) networks. How does data fit change? Overfitting? 2. **Regularization tuning**: Systematically vary regularization weights. Plot loss surface. Find optimal balance. 3. **Input features**: Try different NN inputs: - Just time: β(t) - Just prevalence: β(I/N) - Both: β(I/N, t) - Add S/N: β(S/N, I/N, t) Which works best? 4. **Synthetic data test**: Generate data with known β(t) = β₀ * (1 + 0.3*sin(2πt/20)). Can UDE recover it? 5. **Ensemble uncertainty**: Train 10 UDEs with different random seeds. Plot ensemble of β(t) curves. How variable are they? # Summary **What we learned**: 1. UDEs combine mechanistic structure with learned functions 2. Small datasets (60 points) make UDE training very challenging 3. **Heavy regularization** is essential for sparse data 4. Results are sensitive to hyperparameters ("good luck!") 5. Simpler methods (GPs, splines) often better for small datasets **Key insight**: UDEs are powerful but not magic. They work best when: - You have the right problem (unknown mechanisms) - You have enough data (100s-1000s of points) - You're willing to experiment and tune **Honest conclusion**: For the Tristan da Cunha dataset, a GP-based time-varying β(t) is probably more appropriate than a UDE. But learning to think about hybrid models (mechanistic + ML) is valuable regardless! # Next steps Now that you've seen modern inference methods (variational inference, UDEs), you understand: - **When to use traditional methods** (MCMC, fixed models) - **When to use fast approximations** (VI, mean-field) - **When to use hybrid approaches** (UDEs, GPs) The best modelers know **all** the tools and choose wisely! # References - Rackauckas et al. (2020). "Universal Differential Equations for Scientific Machine Learning." arXiv:2001.04385 - Dandekar et al. (2020). "Neural ODE vs Conventional ODE in COVID-19 Forecasting." NeurIPS Workshop - Rackauckas et al. (2021). "Generalized Physics-Informed Learning Through Language-Wide Differentiable Programming." - DiffEqFlux.jl documentation: https://docs.sciml.ai/DiffEqFlux/ - SciML tutorials: https://docs.sciml.ai/Overview/stable/showcase/missing_physics/
